bytes.topic: Visualization Philosophy — choosing the right chart for the data and audience
bytes.title: Tableau's Chart Selection Philosophy — Matching Marks to Data Structure and Audience
bytes.body:
Tableau's Show Me panel surfaces 24 chart types, but selection should follow data structure, not aesthetic instinct. The core rule: match the mark type to the field combination on your shelves.

Two Dimensions + one Measure → bar or heatmap (drag Measure to Color with SUM aggregation). One date field + one Measure → line chart — only when the date is a continuous green pill; a discrete date pill produces disconnected segment bars, not a trend line. Two Measures on Rows and Columns shelves → scatter plot; add a third Measure to Size for a bubble chart.

Audience context reshapes the decision: executive dashboards favor big-number KPI tiles — use WINDOW_MAX() table calculations for period-over-period delta — and horizontal bars sorted DESC. Analyst workbooks benefit from scatter plots augmented with reference lines via the Analytics pane. For dual-axis charts, right-click the secondary axis and select Synchronize Axis only when both measures share the same scale; mismatched axes mislead silently. Avoid pie charts beyond five marks — Tableau's angle encoding degrades fast, and a sorted bar communicates the same ranking unambiguously.

bytes.topic: Tableau — calculated fields, LOD expressions, dashboard design, performance
bytes.title: Tableau LOD Expressions — FIXED vs INCLUDE vs EXCLUDE and When to Use Each
bytes.body:
LOD expressions let you control the granularity of a calculation independent of the view's level of detail. FIXED computes at a specified dimension regardless of filters — except context filters, which execute before FIXED. INCLUDE adds dimensions to the view's grain, useful when you need a finer calculation than what's on the viz (e.g., average of per-user revenue within a region). EXCLUDE removes a dimension from the current grain — ideal for percent-of-total without table calculations.

Performance tip: FIXED LOD expressions generate a subquery in the SQL sent to your data source. Stacking multiple FIXED LODs on a single viz creates multiple subqueries joined back to the primary. On large extracts or live connections, this compounds fast. Prefer INCLUDE/EXCLUDE when you can — they fold into the main query. For FIXED, use context filters strategically to narrow the subquery early.

Dashboard design: replace table calculations like WINDOW_AVG with FIXED LODs when results need to be stable across different filter selections. Table calcs recompute per partition; LODs are filter-aware only at the dimension level you declare.

bytes.topic: Metabase — questions, dashboards, models, embedding, admin setup
bytes.title: Tableau to Metabase — Questions, Models, and Signed Embedding Mapped for Tableau Users
bytes.body:
Tableau users approaching Metabase need a mental model remap. Metabase's Questions map to Tableau Worksheets — a GUI query builder for simple charts or a raw SQL editor for custom logic. Dashboards work identically: drag questions onto a canvas, wire up cross-filter parameters, and set auto-refresh intervals. Models are the critical divergence: unlike Tableau's Published Data Sources (Hyper-backed, Prep-managed), Metabase Models are SQL-defined semantic layers stored inside the app itself — no ETL tool required. They persist column metadata and serve as trusted sources for downstream Questions, analogous to Tableau's logical table layer.

For embedding, Metabase uses signed JWT tokens rather than Tableau Server's trusted tickets. Enable embedding under Admin → Settings, generate a 256-bit secret, then sign a payload specifying the resource and any locked parameters. The result is an iframe URL — or, with the JS SDK (beta), a React component. Admin setup mirrors Tableau Server site configuration: connect databases via Admin → Databases, apply row-level sandboxing (Pro+) for data security, and map user groups to collection permissions — the equivalent of Tableau's site roles and project-level permissions.
bytes.code_snippet:
import jwt
import time

METABASE_SECRET_KEY = "your-256-bit-secret"
METABASE_SITE_URL = "https://metabase.example.com"

payload = {
    "resource": {"dashboard": 42},
    "params": {"department": ["Engineering"]},
    "exp": round(time.time()) + (60 * 10)  # 10-min expiry
}

token = jwt.encode(payload, METABASE_SECRET_KEY, algorithm="HS256")
iframe_url = f"{METABASE_SITE_URL}/embed/dashboard/{token}#bordered=true&titled=true"
print(iframe_url)

bytes.topic: Apache Superset — datasets, SQL Lab, native filters, chart types, security
bytes.title: Tableau to Apache Superset — Mapping Datasets, SQL Lab, Filters, and RLS
bytes.body:
Tableau's Published Data Sources map directly to Superset's Datasets — a semantic layer defining metrics, dimensions, and Jinja2-templated SQL expressions. Unlike Tableau's drag-and-drop calculated fields, Superset metric expressions (e.g., SUM(revenue)) live in the Dataset editor and are reusable across all charts built on that dataset.

SQL Lab is your Custom SQL replacement: a full query IDE with schema browser, query history, and result caching. Promote any SQL Lab query to a Dataset in one click — no more navigating Edit Connection → Custom SQL in a published workbook.

Native Filters replace Tableau's Filter Actions. They're sidebar-mounted, apply across all in-scope charts, and support cascading dependencies (Region filters State) via the filteredBy config — no Action sheet required.

For Row-Level Security, Superset attaches a SQL clause to a role instead of Tableau's USERNAME() row-level calculated field. The clause (tenant_id = '{{current_username()}}') is evaluated at query time, so role changes take effect immediately — no extract re-publish needed.

Chart type mapping: Tableau's Crosstab → Superset Pivot Table (ECharts-backed); Bullet Graph has no direct equivalent — use Bar Chart with reference lines instead.
bytes.code_snippet:
import requests

BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8088/api/v1"

session = requests.Session()
resp = session.post(f"{BASE_URL}/security/login", json={
    "username": "admin", "password": "admin", "provider": "db"
})
session.headers.update({"Authorization": f"Bearer {resp.json()['access_token']}"})

# Promote a SQL Lab query to a virtual Dataset
payload = {
    "database": 1,
    "sql": "SELECT tenant_id, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY 1",
    "table_name": "orders_by_tenant",
    "schema": "public"
}
result = session.post(f"{BASE_URL}/dataset/", json=payload)
print(result.json()["id"])  # dataset ID — wire this into chart POST /api/v1/chart/

bytes.topic: Grafana — time-series focus, panel types, alerting, provisioning as code
bytes.title: Tableau Server Metrics in Grafana — Time-Series Panels, Alerts & Provisioning as Code
bytes.body: Tableau Server's internal PostgreSQL repository (`workgroup` DB, port 8060) exposes granular session, backgrounder, and VizQL query data that Grafana consumes natively as a time-series datasource. Unlike Tableau's built-in Admin Views — which are themselves Tableau workbooks with fixed granularity — Grafana's Time series panel renders `http_requests.duration_ms` as a true continuous stream with sub-minute resolution. Add a Stat panel for live active session count, a Heatmap for view-load latency distribution across backgrounder workers, and a Bar gauge for extract refresh durations. For alerting, define a Grafana alert rule on `background_jobs` where `finish_code = 1` (failure) exceeds a rolling 5-minute count threshold — this fires before Tableau Server's own Status page reflects the failure. Provision everything as code: drop a datasource YAML and dashboard JSON into `/etc/grafana/provisioning/` and restart. No click-ops, fully version-controlled, and replicable across Tableau Server nodes or forward into a Tableau Cloud migration. This replaces ad-hoc TSM log tailing and manual Admin View exports entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
# /etc/grafana/provisioning/datasources/tableau-repo.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Tableau-Repository
    type: postgres
    url: tableau-server-host:8060
    database: workgroup
    user: tblwgadmin
    secureJsonData:
      password: ${TABLEAU_REPO_PASSWORD}
    jsonData:
      sslmode: require
      postgresVersion: 1400
      timescaledb: false
      timeInterval: "1m"

bytes.topic: Dashboard Design — information hierarchy, reducing cognitive load, data storytelling
bytes.title: Tableau Dashboard Design — Information Hierarchy, Cognitive Load, and Storytelling
bytes.body:
Tableau's information hierarchy lives in Layout Containers. Stack a vertical container with KPI scorecards (fixed 80px height) at top, a trend chart in the middle, and a filtered detail table at the bottom — Summary → Context → Detail. Use tiled containers over floating objects on Tableau Server embeds; tiled layouts respect responsive sizing and avoid z-order collisions.

Reduce cognitive load with Tooltip Sheets: build a mini bar chart, then insert it via Edit Tooltip → Insert Sheet. Dense comparisons appear on hover, keeping the canvas clean.

For storytelling, open with BANs using a calculated field: `(SUM([Sales]) - LOOKUP(SUM([Sales]), -1)) / ABS(LOOKUP(SUM([Sales]), -1))` to show Δ% vs prior period. Flow into trend, then breakdown.

Use Dashboard Actions → Filter Action (not Highlight Action) to preserve hierarchy — highlights retain all marks and introduce noise. Set Inner Padding to 4–8px on containers via Format → Dashboard for consistent breathing room, avoiding blank-object hacks that break on Device Designer layouts.

bytes.topic: Interactivity — filters, drill-downs, cross-filtering, parameterized views
bytes.title: Tableau Interactivity — Parameter Actions, Filter Actions, and Avoiding the Context Re-Query Tax
bytes.body:
Parameter Actions (Tableau 2019.2+) let users update parameter values via click, hover, or menu — no JavaScript needed. The pattern: create a String parameter `[Selected Metric]` with allowable values (Sales, Profit, Quantity), then define a Parameter Action under Dashboard → Actions → Add Action → Change Parameter, targeting that parameter from a dimension field on your chart. Back that with a CASE calculated field:

CASE [Selected Metric]
  WHEN 'Sales' THEN [Sales]
  WHEN 'Profit' THEN [Profit]
  WHEN 'Quantity' THEN [Quantity]
END

Every axis, color encoding, and tooltip reference that field — one click rewires the entire dashboard. For cross-sheet filtering, use Filter Actions (source → target sheet, trigger: Select) rather than stacking Context Filters. Context Filters force a full re-execution of the underlying query, turning an O(1) mark selection into a blocking scan. Prefer Set Actions for multi-sheet coordination when the filtered set is reused across views — sets are computed once and referenced by name, keeping extracts warm. Reserve Context Filters strictly for narrowing fixed-row extracts at load time, not for runtime interactivity.

bytes.topic: Data Freshness — scheduled refreshes, caching strategies, SLA on dashboard lag
bytes.title: Tableau Data Freshness — Extract Schedules, Cache Layers, and SLA Monitoring
bytes.body:
Tableau dashboards silently serve stale data when extract refresh schedules aren't tuned. Tableau offers two connection modes: live (queries the source on every interaction) and extract (.hyper file, queried by the in-process Hyper engine). Extracts are significantly faster but introduce lag equal to your refresh cadence.

On Tableau Server/Cloud, scheduled refreshes run as background tasks — configurable per datasource under Data Sources › Schedule. For sub-hourly SLAs, use incremental refreshes instead of full extracts: incremental scans only new rows via an Incremental Refresh Key column, cutting refresh time from minutes to seconds on large sources.

Tableau's caching stack has two layers: the VizQL query cache (shared across users, TTL tunable via tsm configuration set -k vizqlserver.queryCacheMaxSize) and the Hyper model cache (the .hyper file itself). After each refresh completes, hit /views/{id}/image via the REST API to warm the VizQL cache before business hours.

Monitor SLA compliance via datasource.updated_at from the REST API — alert when now() - updated_at exceeds your threshold. Failed background jobs don't surface to end users; polling this field catches silent failures before stakeholders notice stale metrics on production dashboards.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tableauserverclient as TSC
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

server = TSC.Server("https://your-tableau-server", use_server_version=True)
auth = TSC.PersonalAccessTokenAuth("token-name", "token-secret", "site-id")
SLA_HOURS = 4

with server.auth.sign_in(auth):
    all_datasources, _ = server.datasources.get()
    for ds in all_datasources:
        lag = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - ds.updated_at
        if lag > timedelta(hours=SLA_HOURS):
            print(f"SLA BREACH: {ds.name} last refreshed {lag} ago — triggering refresh")
            server.datasources.refresh(ds)

bytes.topic: Access Control — row-level security in BI tools, embedding with user context
bytes.title: Tableau Row-Level Security — Enforcing User Context in Embedded Dashboards
bytes.body:
Tableau's row-level security hinges on the USERNAME() function paired with a data source filter. Create a calculated field matching a [user_email] column in your entitlements table, then add a data source filter: USERNAME() = [user_email] or ISMEMBEROF("region-west") for group-based access. Every VizQL query Tableau generates appends this filter automatically — users never see out-of-scope rows regardless of URL manipulation.

For embedded analytics, the problem is binding the viewer's real identity to USERNAME(). Tableau Connected Apps solve this: your backend mints a short-lived JWT signed with the Connected App secret, setting the viewer's Tableau Server username in the sub claim. Pass that token to the <tableau-viz> web component's token attribute. Tableau Server validates the signature, resolves sub to a server account, and USERNAME() propagates correctly through every viz on the page.

Avoid the initialSQL user-attribute pattern for RLS — it filters at the database layer and silently breaks with extracts. Data source filters backed by an entitlements table work with both live connections and published extracts, and survive data source promotions across dev/prod environments.
bytes.code_snippet:
import jwt, time, uuid

CLIENT_ID = "your-connected-app-client-id"
SECRET_ID = "your-secret-id"
SECRET_VALUE = "your-secret-value"

payload = {
    "iss": CLIENT_ID,
    "exp": int(time.time()) + 300,
    "jti": str(uuid.uuid4()),
    "aud": "tableau",
    "sub": "alice@company.com",
    "scp": ["tableau:views:embed"]
}

token = jwt.encode(
    payload,
    SECRET_VALUE,
    algorithm="HS256",
    headers={"kid": SECRET_ID, "iss": CLIENT_ID}
)

bytes.topic: Performance — slow dashboards, materialized views, extract vs live connection
bytes.title: Tableau Performance — Extracts, Materialized Views, and Why Your Dashboard Is Slow
bytes.body:
Live connections query your database on every interaction — every filter change, every mark selection fires a new SQL round-trip. For high-cardinality dimensions or complex joins, this kills response time. Switch to a Tableau Extract (.hyper) and Hyper's columnar engine handles aggregations in-process, often 10–50x faster than live OLAP queries.

Before reaching for an extract, check your data source first. In Tableau Desktop, open the Performance Recorder (Help → Settings and Performance → Start Performance Recording), interact with the slow view, then stop it. The resulting workbook shows exact query durations — isolate whether the bottleneck is query execution, rendering, or geocoding.

For live connections you can't extract (real-time compliance, row-level security via initial SQL), push the heavy lifting to the database. Create a materialized view over your fact-dimension joins and point Tableau's Custom SQL to it. Use context filters to reduce the initial data scope before dimension filters execute — context filters generate a temp table Tableau queries first, cutting subsequent query size dramatically. Avoid table calculations on large datasets; they run post-query in Tableau's engine, not the DB.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Materialized view for a slow Tableau live connection
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_sales_summary AS
SELECT
    d.region,
    d.category,
    d.order_date::DATE AS order_date,
    SUM(f.sales)        AS total_sales,
    SUM(f.profit)       AS total_profit,
    COUNT(f.order_id)   AS order_count
FROM fact_orders f
JOIN dim_product d ON f.product_key = d.product_key
GROUP BY d.region, d.category, d.order_date::DATE;

-- Refresh on a schedule aligned to Tableau extract refresh cadence
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY mv_sales_summary;

bytes.topic: Mobile Dashboards — responsive design, what works on small screens
bytes.title: Tableau Mobile Dashboards — What Actually Works on Small Screens
bytes.body: Tableau's Device Designer (Dashboard → Device Layouts) lets you create Phone and Tablet layouts from a single workbook. Each layout is independent — you can hide sheets, reorder objects, and resize containers without touching the Default view. Use Automatic sizing on the Phone layout, not Fixed; Fixed layouts force horizontal scrolling on most devices. Tiled layouts outperform floating on mobile — floating objects don't reflow, so they overlap unpredictably below 375px width. Strip non-essential views: if a bar chart and a summary KPI tile convey the same insight, keep only the KPI on Phone layout. Use Show/Hide Button containers to collapse filters rather than stacking them vertically. Avoid dense crosstabs entirely — they're unusable on touch screens. For marks, bump minimum mark size to 14px via Format → Marks to hit touch targets. Dashboard Actions using Highlight (not Filter) preserve context without navigating away, which is critical when back-navigation on mobile is expensive. Publish to Tableau Server/Cloud and test via the Tableau Mobile app — the browser preview in Desktop lies about touch behavior.

bytes.topic: Embedding — SSO embedding, white-labeling, iFrame vs SDK approaches
bytes.title: Tableau Embedding API v3 — SSO, White-Labeling, and Ditching iFrames
bytes.body: Tableau's legacy iFrame embed passes a `?:embed=yes` URL param and relies on Trusted Authentication — a server-to-server ticket exchange that's brittle and deprecated. The modern path is the Embedding API v3 (`@tableau/embedding-api`), which exposes a `<tableau-viz>` web component backed by a proper JavaScript SDK. For SSO, replace Trusted Auth tickets with Connected Apps JWTs: register a Connected App in Tableau Server/Cloud, sign a HS256 JWT with `sub` (username), `scp: ['tableau:views:embed']`, `iss` (client ID), and `kid` (secret ID). Pass it as the `token` attribute — no server round-trip per user, just token refresh. White-labeling is handled via `toolbar="hidden"`, `hideTabs`, and `customViews="hidden"` attributes directly on the component. For deeper CSS control, Tableau Cloud supports Custom Fonts and zone-level styling, but DOM injection into the viz iframe is blocked by CSP. Critically, Embedding API v3 exposes `addEventListener` for `FilterChanged`, `MarksSelected`, and `FirstInteractive` — none of which are accessible in raw iFrame mode. Use the SDK; iFrame embedding is a dead end.

bytes.topic: Self-serve Analytics — enabling non-technical users, guardrails, semantic layer
bytes.title: Tableau Self-Serve Analytics — Certified Sources, RLS Guardrails & the Semantic Layer
bytes.body:
Tableau's semantic layer lives in the Published Data Source on Tableau Cloud or Server. Certify a source (web UI or REST API via Tableau Server Client) to surface it first in Ask Data and Tableau Pulse searches and signal governed trustworthiness to business users.

Embed business logic as named calculated fields — `[Gross Margin %]` instead of raw division scattered across workbooks. Users drag pre-validated metrics; drift is impossible. Row-level security runs at query time as a boolean Data Source Filter using `USERNAME()` and `ISMEMBEROF()`: one filter on the published source covers every downstream workbook automatically.

For Pulse's natural-language layer, field naming and synonyms (Data > Synonyms in Tableau Desktop before publishing) directly govern response accuracy — garbage field names produce garbage Pulse answers. Lock sensitive dimensions with column-level permissions on Tableau Cloud so analysts see the field exists but cannot query its values.

The governed self-serve pattern: certify the source → encode logic as named calcs → apply `USERNAME()`/`ISMEMBEROF()` Data Source Filter → set synonyms → expose via Pulse. Non-technical users get guardrailed exploration; the semantic layer ensures no rogue metric definitions slip through.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tableauserverclient as TSC

server = TSC.Server("https://your-site.online.tableau.com", use_server_version=True)
auth = TSC.PersonalAccessTokenAuth("token-name", "token-secret", "site-id")

with server.auth.sign_in(auth):
    datasource = server.datasources.get_by_id("<datasource-luid>")
    datasource.certified = True
    datasource.certification_note = "Approved by Data Team — single source of truth for Revenue metrics"
    server.datasources.update(datasource)
    print(f"Certified '{datasource.name}' on {datasource.updated_at}")

bytes.topic: Metrics Layer — single source of truth for metrics, dbt Metrics, Cube
bytes.title: Tableau + Cube Metrics Layer — One Definition, Zero Drift Across Workbooks
bytes.body:
Scattered calculated fields across Tableau workbooks are a metrics consistency nightmare — three dashboards, three definitions of 'revenue'. A semantic metrics layer centralizes that logic outside Tableau entirely.

Cube.dev exposes a Postgres-compatible SQL API that Tableau connects to via the built-in PostgreSQL connector (host: your Cube Cloud endpoint, port 5432). Once live, Cube cubes appear as tables in Tableau's Data Source pane — measures like `revenue` and `avg_order_value` surface as pre-aggregated fields. No LOD expressions needed for standard business KPIs.

dbt's Semantic Layer (MetricFlow) works similarly: point Tableau at the dbt Semantic Layer JDBC driver (supported from Tableau Desktop 2023.2+), authenticate via your dbt Cloud environment ID, and MetricFlow-defined metrics become queryable columns. Both paths mean analysts drag consistent, governance-approved measures instead of reimplementing `SUM(IF([status]='completed',[amount],0))` per workbook.

The Cube schema below defines `revenue` and `avg_order_value` once. Every Tableau workbook connecting to that endpoint queries identical logic — no Published Data Source sprawl, no metric drift.

bytes.topic: Alerting — threshold alerts, anomaly detection, notification channels
bytes.title: Tableau Alerting — Threshold Alerts, Pulse Anomaly Detection, and Webhook Channels
bytes.body:
Tableau's data-driven alerts fire when a mark's value crosses a condition you define directly on a published view. Open any viz on Tableau Server or Cloud, click the bell icon on an axis, set your operator (greater than, less than, equal to), and Tableau polls the underlying data source on your chosen frequency. Recipients get an email with a snapshot of the triggering view—no ETL pipeline required.

For anomaly detection without manual threshold tuning, Tableau Pulse (Cloud-only) uses Einstein AI to monitor connected metrics and surface unexpected shifts proactively. It delivers digests to Slack or email when values deviate from learned baselines—no calculated field gymnastics needed.

For custom notification routing, Tableau Server and Cloud expose webhooks that fire on events like `datasource-refresh-succeeded` and `view-created`. POST these payloads to a relay service to fan out into PagerDuty, Microsoft Teams, or custom Slack channels. The `tableauserverclient` (TSC) Python library gives you programmatic control over data alerts—critical when migrating alert rules across dozens of workbooks or auditing which alerts are still actively owned.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tableauserverclient as TSC

server = TSC.Server("https://your-tableau-server", use_server_version=True)
auth = TSC.PersonalAccessTokenAuth("token_name", "token_secret", site_id="your-site")

with server.auth.sign_in(auth):
    all_alerts, _ = server.data_alerts.get()

    for alert in all_alerts:
        print(f"{alert.subject} | owner={alert.owner_id} | freq={alert.frequency}")

    # Prune alerts owned by departed users or marked deprecated
    stale = [a for a in all_alerts if "deprecated" in a.subject.lower()]
    for alert in stale:
        server.data_alerts.delete(alert.id)
        print(f"Deleted: {alert.subject}")

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Tableau vs Looker vs Metabase vs Superset decision framework
bytes.title: Tableau vs Looker vs Metabase vs Superset — A Tableau User's Decision Framework
bytes.body:
Reach for Tableau when your analysis demands LOD expressions — FIXED calculates independently of view dimensions, something Metabase's SQL-question model and Superset's metric layer can't replicate without raw SQL acrobatics. Looker comes closest via symmetric_aggregates in LookML, but requires an engineer to own the semantic layer full-time.

Decision heuristics:
- Tableau: Complex aggregations (FIXED/INCLUDE/EXCLUDE LOD), pixel-perfect branded dashboards, Tableau Prep for visual ETL, row-level security via USERNAME() and ISMEMBEROF() calculated fields, cross-database blending without a warehouse join
- Looker: Governed semantic layer (LookML) as single source of truth, embedded analytics via signed explore URLs, teams with dedicated LookML engineers
- Metabase: Non-technical stakeholders (product/ops), sub-5-min self-service setup, SQL questions sufficient, cost-constrained teams
- Superset: Engineering-led orgs, open-source mandate, custom Echarts plugins, Trino/Presto-heavy stack

The decisive Tableau edge: .hyper extracts deliver sub-second query times on 50M-row datasets without touching your warehouse — Superset and Metabase issue live queries every render.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tableauserverclient as TSC

server = TSC.Server("https://your-site.online.tableau.com", use_server_version=True)
auth = TSC.PersonalAccessTokenAuth("token_name", "token_secret", site_id="your-site")

with server.auth.sign_in(auth):
    all_ds, _ = server.datasources.get()
    target = next(ds for ds in all_ds if ds.name == "Revenue_Certified")
    # Certify the datasource — Tableau's governed semantic layer answer to LookML
    target.certified = True
    target.certification_note = "Approved by Data Governance — single source of truth"
    server.datasources.update(target)
    print(f"Certified: {target.name} | Project: {target.project_name}")
